comment_text.sn12.63:1.2: On the four fuels see SN 12.11. Here the Buddha drives home the suffering of dependency with a series of unforgettable similes.
comment_text.sn12.63:2.9: In the Mahābhārata, Bhīṣma forcefully condemned meat eating, saying, “The meat of other animals is like the flesh of one’s child” (13.115.010c; cf. 13.148.017c). Some, however, believed a child’s flesh had magic potency (Kathāsaritsāgara 29d).
comment_text.sn12.63:3.8: This reflection goes as far as non-return, because the focus is on the elimination of sense desire.
comment_text.sn12.63:4.2: Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 3.1.2.13–17 explains that the cow was once naked, so the gods flayed man and clothed the cow with it. Now a man bleeds when cut even by grass, whereas a cow endures rain and cold and heat.
comment_text.sn12.63:5.2: Such pits are frequently mentioned in the suttas and must have been a common sight. Since fired bricks were not regularly produced, perhaps they were used for metals or pottery, although kilns are also mentioned. Falling in one is an apt simile for the pains of hell (AN 7.66:8.3, SN 12.51:12.1).
comment_text.sn12.63:6.23: This too is in reference to the sufferings undergone by consciousness in transmigration, especially in the hells (MN 129:8.2, SN 56.35).

root_text.sn12.63:1.2: “cattārome, bhikkhave, āhārā bhūtānaṁ vā sattānaṁ ṭhitiyā sambhavesīnaṁ vā anuggahāya.
root_text.sn12.63:1.4: Kabaḷīkāro āhāro oḷāriko vā sukhumo vā, phasso dutiyo, manosañcetanā tatiyā, viññāṇaṁ catutthaṁ.
root_text.sn12.63:1.5: Ime kho, bhikkhave, cattāro āhārā bhūtānaṁ vā sattānaṁ ṭhitiyā sambhavesīnaṁ vā anuggahāya.
root_text.sn12.63:2.2: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, dve jāyampatikā parittaṁ sambalaṁ ādāya kantāramaggaṁ paṭipajjeyyuṁ.
root_text.sn12.63:2.4: Atha kho tesaṁ, bhikkhave, dvinnaṁ jāyampatikānaṁ kantāragatānaṁ yā parittā sambalamattā, sā parikkhayaṁ pariyādānaṁ gaccheyya.
root_text.sn12.63:2.6: Atha kho tesaṁ, bhikkhave, dvinnaṁ jāyampatikānaṁ evamassa:
root_text.sn12.63:2.7: ‘amhākaṁ kho yā parittā sambalamattā sā parikkhīṇā pariyādiṇṇā.
root_text.sn12.63:2.9: Yannūna mayaṁ imaṁ ekaputtakaṁ piyaṁ manāpaṁ vadhitvā vallūrañca soṇḍikañca karitvā puttamaṁsāni khādantā evaṁ taṁ kantārāvasesaṁ nitthareyyāma, mā sabbeva tayo vinassimhā’ti.
root_text.sn12.63:2.10: Atha kho te, bhikkhave, dve jāyampatikā taṁ ekaputtakaṁ piyaṁ manāpaṁ vadhitvā vallūrañca soṇḍikañca karitvā puttamaṁsāni khādantā evaṁ taṁ kantārāvasesaṁ nitthareyyuṁ.
root_text.sn12.63:2.11: Te puttamaṁsāni ceva khādeyyuṁ, ure ca paṭipiseyyuṁ:
root_text.sn12.63:3.2: api nu te davāya vā āhāraṁ āhāreyyuṁ, madāya vā āhāraṁ āhāreyyuṁ, maṇḍanāya vā āhāraṁ āhāreyyuṁ, vibhūsanāya vā āhāraṁ āhāreyyun”ti?
root_text.sn12.63:3.4: “Nanu te, bhikkhave, yāvadeva kantārassa nittharaṇatthāya āhāraṁ āhāreyyun”ti?
root_text.sn12.63:3.6: “‘Evameva khvāhaṁ, bhikkhave, kabaḷīkāro āhāro daṭṭhabbo’ti vadāmi.
root_text.sn12.63:3.7: Kabaḷīkāre, bhikkhave, āhāre pariññāte pañca kāmaguṇiko rāgo pariññāto hoti.
root_text.sn12.63:3.8: Pañca kāmaguṇike rāge pariññāte natthi taṁ saṁyojanaṁ yena saṁyojanena saṁyutto ariyasāvako puna imaṁ lokaṁ āgaccheyya.
root_text.sn12.63:4.2: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, gāvī niccammā kuṭṭañce nissāya tiṭṭheyya. Ye kuṭṭanissitā pāṇā te naṁ khādeyyuṁ.
root_text.sn12.63:4.3: Rukkhañce nissāya tiṭṭheyya, ye rukkhanissitā pāṇā te naṁ khādeyyuṁ.
root_text.sn12.63:4.4: Udakañce nissāya tiṭṭheyya, ye udakanissitā pāṇā te naṁ khādeyyuṁ.
root_text.sn12.63:4.5: Ākāsañce nissāya tiṭṭheyya, ye ākāsanissitā pāṇā te naṁ khādeyyuṁ.
root_text.sn12.63:4.6: Yaṁ yadeva hi sā, bhikkhave, gāvī niccammā nissāya tiṭṭheyya, ye tannissitā pāṇā te naṁ khādeyyuṁ.
root_text.sn12.63:4.8: Phasse, bhikkhave, āhāre pariññāte tisso vedanā pariññātā honti.
root_text.sn12.63:4.9: Tīsu vedanāsu pariññātāsu ariyasāvakassa natthi kiñci uttarikaraṇīyanti vadāmi.
root_text.sn12.63:5.2: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, aṅgārakāsu sādhikaporisā puṇṇā aṅgārānaṁ vītaccikānaṁ vītadhūmānaṁ.
root_text.sn12.63:5.3: Atha puriso āgaccheyya jīvitukāmo amaritukāmo sukhakāmo dukkhappaṭikūlo.
root_text.sn12.63:5.4: Tamenaṁ dve balavanto purisā nānābāhāsu gahetvā taṁ aṅgārakāsuṁ upakaḍḍheyyuṁ.
root_text.sn12.63:5.5: Atha kho, bhikkhave, tassa purisassa ārakāvassa cetanā ārakā patthanā ārakā paṇidhi.
root_text.sn12.63:5.8: ‘imañcāhaṁ aṅgārakāsuṁ papatissāmi, tatonidānaṁ maraṇaṁ vā nigacchāmi maraṇamattaṁ vā dukkhan’ti.
root_text.sn12.63:5.10: Manosañcetanāya, bhikkhave, āhāre pariññāte tisso taṇhā pariññātā honti.
root_text.sn12.63:5.11: Tīsu taṇhāsu pariññātāsu ariyasāvakassa natthi kiñci uttarikaraṇīyanti vadāmi.
root_text.sn12.63:6.2: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, coraṁ āgucāriṁ gahetvā rañño dasseyyuṁ:
root_text.sn12.63:6.3: ‘ayaṁ te, deva, coro āgucārī, imassa yaṁ icchasi taṁ daṇḍaṁ paṇehī’ti.
root_text.sn12.63:6.5: ‘gacchatha, bho, imaṁ purisaṁ pubbaṇhasamayaṁ sattisatena hanathā’ti.
root_text.sn12.63:6.11: ‘gacchatha, bho, taṁ purisaṁ majjhanhikasamayaṁ sattisatena hanathā’ti.
root_text.sn12.63:6.17: ‘gacchatha, bho, taṁ purisaṁ sāyanhasamayaṁ sattisatena hanathā’ti.
root_text.sn12.63:6.20: api nu so puriso divasaṁ tīhi sattisatehi haññamāno tatonidānaṁ dukkhaṁ domanassaṁ paṭisaṁvediyethā”ti?
root_text.sn12.63:6.21: “Ekissāpi, bhante, sattiyā haññamāno tatonidānaṁ dukkhaṁ domanassaṁ paṭisaṁvediyetha;
root_text.sn12.63:6.22: ko pana vādo tīhi sattisatehi haññamāno”ti.
root_text.sn12.63:6.24: Viññāṇe, bhikkhave, āhāre pariññāte nāmarūpaṁ pariññātaṁ hoti, nāmarūpe pariññāte ariyasāvakassa natthi kiñci uttarikaraṇīyanti vadāmī”ti.

translation_text.sn12.63:1.2: “Mendicants, these four fuels maintain sentient beings that have been born and help those about to be born.
translation_text.sn12.63:1.4: Edible food, whether solid or subtle; contact is the second, mental intention the third, and consciousness the fourth.
translation_text.sn12.63:1.5: These are the four fuels that maintain sentient beings that have been born and help those about to be born.
translation_text.sn12.63:2.1: And how should you regard edible food?
translation_text.sn12.63:2.2: Suppose a couple who were husband and wife set out to cross a desert, taking limited supplies.
translation_text.sn12.63:2.3: They had an only child, dear and beloved.
translation_text.sn12.63:2.4: As the couple were crossing the desert their limited quantity of supplies would run out,
translation_text.sn12.63:2.5: and they’d still have the rest of the desert to cross.
translation_text.sn12.63:2.6: Then it would occur to that couple:
translation_text.sn12.63:2.7: ‘Our limited quantity of supplies has run out,
translation_text.sn12.63:2.8: and we still have the rest of the desert to cross.
translation_text.sn12.63:2.9: Why don’t we kill our only child, so dear and beloved, and prepare dried and spiced meat? Then we can make it across the desert by eating our child’s flesh. Let not all three perish.’
translation_text.sn12.63:2.10: Then that couple would kill their only child, so dear and beloved, and prepare dried and spiced meat. They’d make it across the desert by eating their child’s flesh.
translation_text.sn12.63:2.11: And as they’d eat their child’s flesh, they’d beat their breasts and cry:
translation_text.sn12.63:2.12: ‘Where are you, our only child? Where are you, our only child?’
translation_text.sn12.63:3.2: Would they eat that food for fun, indulgence, adornment, or decoration?”
translation_text.sn12.63:3.4: “Wouldn’t they eat that food just so they could make it across the desert?”
translation_text.sn12.63:3.6: “I say that this is how you should regard edible food.
translation_text.sn12.63:3.7: When edible food is completely understood, desire for the five kinds of sensual stimulation is completely understood.
translation_text.sn12.63:3.8: When desire for the five kinds of sensual stimulation is completely understood, a noble disciple is bound by no fetter that might return them again to this world.
translation_text.sn12.63:4.1: And how should you regard contact as fuel?
translation_text.sn12.63:4.2: Suppose there was a flayed cow. If she stands by a wall, the creatures on the wall bite her.
translation_text.sn12.63:4.3: If she stands under a tree, the creatures in the tree bite her.
translation_text.sn12.63:4.4: If she stands in some water, the creatures in the water bite her.
translation_text.sn12.63:4.5: If she stands in the open, the creatures in the open bite her.
translation_text.sn12.63:4.6: Wherever that flayed cow stands, the creatures there would bite her.
translation_text.sn12.63:4.7: I say that this is how you should regard contact as fuel.
translation_text.sn12.63:4.8: When contact as fuel is completely understood, the three feelings are completely understood.
translation_text.sn12.63:4.9: When the three feelings are completely understood, a noble disciple has nothing further to do, I say.
translation_text.sn12.63:5.1: And how should you regard mental intention as fuel?
translation_text.sn12.63:5.2: Suppose there was a pit of glowing coals deeper than a man’s height, filled with glowing coals that neither flamed nor smoked.
translation_text.sn12.63:5.3: Then a person would come along who wants to live and doesn’t want to die, who wants to be happy and recoils from pain.
translation_text.sn12.63:5.4: Two strong men would grab them by the arms and drag them towards the pit of glowing coals.
translation_text.sn12.63:5.5: Then that person’s intention, aim, and wish would be to get far away.
translation_text.sn12.63:5.8: ‘If I fall in that pit of glowing coals, that will result in my death or deadly pain.’
translation_text.sn12.63:5.9: I say that this is how you should regard mental intention as fuel.
translation_text.sn12.63:5.10: When mental intention as fuel is completely understood, the three cravings are completely understood.
translation_text.sn12.63:5.11: When the three cravings are completely understood, a noble disciple has nothing further to do, I say.
translation_text.sn12.63:6.1: And how should you regard consciousness as fuel?
translation_text.sn12.63:6.2: Suppose they were to arrest a bandit, a criminal and present him to the king, saying:
translation_text.sn12.63:6.3: ‘Your Majesty, this is a bandit, a criminal. Punish him as you will.’
translation_text.sn12.63:6.5: ‘Go, my men, and strike this man in the morning with a hundred spears!’
translation_text.sn12.63:6.6: The king’s men did as they were told.
translation_text.sn12.63:6.7: Then at midday the king would say:
translation_text.sn12.63:6.11: ‘Go, my men, and strike this man in the middle of the day with a hundred spears!’
translation_text.sn12.63:6.12: The king’s men did as they were told.
translation_text.sn12.63:6.13: Then late in the afternoon the king would say:
translation_text.sn12.63:6.17: ‘Go, my men, and strike this man in the late afternoon with a hundred spears!’
translation_text.sn12.63:6.18: The king’s men did as they were told.
translation_text.sn12.63:6.20: Would that man experience pain and distress from being struck with three hundred spears a day?”
translation_text.sn12.63:6.21: “Sir, that man would experience pain and distress from being struck with one spear,
translation_text.sn12.63:6.23: “I say that this is how you should regard consciousness as fuel.
translation_text.sn12.63:6.24: When consciousness as fuel is completely understood, name and form is completely understood. When name and form are completely understood, a noble disciple has nothing further to do, I say.”

variant_text.sn12.63:2.7: pariyādiṇṇā → pariyādinnā (bj, sya-all, km); pariyādānaṁ (pts1ed, pts2ed)
variant_text.sn12.63:2.8: anittiṇṇo → anitthiṇṇo (sya-all, km); anithiṇṇo (pts1ed, pts2ed); anatiṇṇo (mr)
variant_text.sn12.63:4.2: kuṭṭañce → kuḍḍañce (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed, pts2ed)
variant_text.sn12.63:4.6: nissāya tiṭṭheyya, ye tannissitā → nissāya tiṭṭheyya ye tannissitā tannissitā (bj, pts1ed-2), nissāya tiṭṭheyya ye taṁ nissitā taṁ nissitā (sya-all, km)

reference_text.sn12.63:1.1: ms12S2_449, msdiv63, ndp13.84, pts-vp-pli2.98, sya16.119, vri24.87
